items.q: Why is burning fossil fuels considered to disrupt the carbon cycle?
items.correct: It releases carbon stored for millions of years much faster than the cycle can absorb.
items.misconception: It creates new carbon that did not exist before.
items.why: Students think combustion creates carbon. It RELEASES previously stored carbon - the issue is rate. Geological storage took millions of years; we're releasing it in centuries, overwhelming natural sinks.

items.correct: The maximum population an environment can sustainably support given available resources.
items.misconception: How much an animal can physically carry.
items.random_wrong: The size a species can reach.
items.why: Students take 'carrying' literally. It's an ECOLOGICAL concept - the ceiling set by food, water, space, etc. Populations may temporarily exceed it but then crash, or oscillate around it.

items.q: What is the leading driver of global deforestation?
items.correct: Agriculture (especially cattle ranching, soy, palm oil).
items.why: Students think trees are felled for wood. While logging contributes, the largest driver is land conversion for agriculture - food production drives most tropical forest loss, especially commodities like beef and palm oil.
Correct answer: Solar. “Coal” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Spinning a generator. “Burning gas” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many main renewable types were listed (solar, wind, hydro, geothermal, biomass)?
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
